message:
chore(beads): file 5 codebase-audit gap beads — FINAL skill in audit rotation (cc_1)

Applied /codebase-audit as the final audit skill in the rotation.
Surveyed the 9 named domains (capture/storage, patterns, mux/term/
codec, robot/MCP, workflows, policy/redactor, runtime/ingest,
telemetry, fleet/replay), identified the highest-value gap PER
DOMAIN that wasn't caught by the prior 10 audits (reality-check,
conformance, golden-artifacts, fuzzing, profiling, modes-of-
reasoning, security-audit, multi-pass-bug-hunting, plus the
implicit no-mocks/deadlock/mock-finder rotations).

Filed 5 of 9 (other 4 domains had concentrated coverage from
prior audits or no clearly-uncaught gap):

ft-ctt7k  [capture/storage] WAL checkpoint cost under sustained
          writes is unmeasured. SQLite auto-checkpoint stalls
          synchronously on the writer thread; ft has no PRAGMA
          configuration knobs (wal_autocheckpoint,
          synchronous, journal_size_limit) and no
          checkpoint-aware backpressure tier. Different from
          ft-9r82k recorder ingest bench: this measures the
          DB-side stall, not the serialize/flush cost.

ft-7v53r  [mux/term/codec] mux pool socket-disappearance fault
          injection is missing. Existing mux benches cover
          happy-path scaling; no test exercises socket file
          deletion / mux server SIGKILL / atomic-rename
          mid-RPC. Pool exhaustion via stuck-broken-connections
          is the realistic operational hazard.

ft-pe4ds  [robot/MCP] error-envelope schema is unvalidated. ft-5ikbd
          shipped runtime validation but only loads the
          `success_envelope` field from each fixture. The 10+
          MCP_ERR_* error_code constants in mcp_tools.rs have
          no fixture coverage and no validator gate. Concrete
          fix proposed: per-error fixture corpus + production-
          variant→fixture coverage assertion.

ft-3p7re  [workflows] no overall workflow deadline. WorkflowRunnerConfig
          has step_timeout_ms (30s) but no total_timeout_ms.
          Pathological retry loops or wedged WaitCondition::External
          can pin a pane indefinitely. Sibling to ft-ao9k9 (the
          external-signal wait fix this bead is the deadline
          for) and ft-qkd2f (PaneLockGuard, the lock-leak fix
          this bead drives).

ft-5cl1b  [fleet/replay] cross-architecture replay determinism
          unverified. README markets "deterministic replay" but
          replay_decision_diff_golden.rs only covers single-host.
          Six concrete nondeterminism sources (FP ordering, HashMap
          iteration, Instant resolution, PIDs, Unicode-width
          tables, zstd version drift) — none gated. Concrete fix:
          determinism-contract doc + scrubber library + macos-
          captures-ubuntu-replays CI matrix.

Domains where prior audits already had concentrated coverage:

- patterns: ft-b35tw (pack format spec) + ft-8hbq8 (YAML/JSON fuzz)
  + the 6 existing pattern fuzz targets exhaust the high-leverage
  gaps. Hot-reload atomicity is the next step but lower-priority.
- policy/redactor: ft-3xek9 (redactor blocklist) + ft-3se13
  (decision log secrets) + ft-j0ufc (workflow privilege amp) cover
  the principal abuse paths. Rule precedence is documented in
  source comments (deny > require_approval > allow); no defect.
- runtime/ingest: ft-3tvvt (block_on / async-vs-sync) + ft-hfbsp
  (simd_scan fuzz) + the existing scan_pipeline + simd_scan benches
  cover the principal gradient. Backpressure hysteresis would be
  next; not high-leverage.
- telemetry: lower priority — fleet-scale cardinality is real but
  doesn't have a concrete defect-shape today; would file as
  observability follow-on, not gap-bead.

Audit rotation complete. Total beads filed across 11 audit skills
this session: ~50 high-quality beads + several closures for
in-flight work. Standing by for next dispatch.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.7 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>

 {"id":"ft-3l5bu","title":"[MEDIUM] RateLimiter.pane_counts HashMap leaks entries for every (pane_id, action) pair that is ever checked","description":"# Defect

## File + line

`crates/frankenterm-core/src/policy.rs:2103` — `RateLimiter::check`.

## Concrete failure path

Every `check(action, Some(pane_id))` call executes

    let timestamps = self.pane_counts.entry((pane_id, action)).or_default();

which **always** inserts a `Vec<Instant>` under `(pane_id, action)` if it doesn't exist, before any limit logic runs. Same story for `global_counts` keyed by `ActionKind`.

After a pane is destroyed, nothing removes its entries. `prune_old(timestamps, window_start)` removes expired timestamps from the Vec but leaves the Vec itself and the HashMap entry in place. Over the lifetime of a long-running `PolicyEngine`, the HashMap grows monotonically:

* Bound per pane: at most one entry per `ActionKind` variant (finite enum).
* Bound across panes: `unique(pane_id) × |ActionKind|`.

On a 200+-pane swarm churning through ~1000 panes/day over 90 days, that's ~90k × |ActionKind| entries at ~16 bytes key + baseline Vec — not a crash, but a monotonic leak tracked nowhere. **Same class as ft-l6v1r** (`pane_activity_tracker`) and the `BackpressureMetrics::dropped_by_pane` leak I fixed earlier (f2390da9).

## Worse-case path: pane_id reuse

The ft-l6v1r commit observed that wezterm can reuse pane_ids. If a reused pane_id hashes to an old (pane_id, action) entry whose Vec still contains stale timestamps, the new pane inherits the dead pane's rate-limit budget — either under-counted (if the prune pass has already emptied the Vec) or over-counted (if timestamps haven't aged out yet). The current `check` path prunes based on `window_start` which is relative to `Instant::now()`, so stale timestamps from a destroyed pane will age out correctly — but entries without any timestamps will still mis-attribute the **first** rate-limited hit back to the old pane's history in any diagnostic that reads `pane_counts` directly.

## Fix scope

1. `RateLimiter::cleanup_pane(pane_id) -> usize` — remove every `(pane_id, _)` entry from `pane_counts`, return the number removed for telemetry / debug_assert. Cheap: one full iteration.
2. Unit test pinning: entries present before, zero after, unrelated panes untouched, idempotent on panes never inserted.
3. Wiring into the PaneDestroyed teardown path is a separate follow-up bead — `PolicyEngine` is not currently plumbed through `ObservationRuntime::spawn_discovery_task` or `handle_native_event`, and that plumbing is a broader cross-boundary change that belongs on its own bead.

+{"id":"ft-3p7re","title":"[codebase-audit][workflows] no overall workflow deadline — step_timeout_ms only; pathological retry loops can pin a pane forever","description":"## Domain gap

`crates/frankenterm-core/src/workflows/runner.rs:218-237`:

```rust
pub struct WorkflowRunnerConfig {
    pub max_concurrent: usize,         // 3
    pub step_timeout_ms: u64,          // 30_000
    pub retry_backoff_multiplier: f64, // 2.0
    pub max_retries_per_step: usize,   // 3
}
```

The runner caps **per-step** timeout (30s) and **per-step**
retry count (3). It does NOT cap **total workflow runtime**.

Concrete arithmetic: a workflow with N steps, M retries each,
exponential backoff:

- 10 steps × 3 retries × (1 + 2 + 4)s = 210s minimum runtime
- + 30s timeout per step × 10 = 300s additional ceiling
- = up to **510 seconds** for a "well-behaved" workflow

For a pathological workflow with longer step counts, custom
retry policies, or `WaitCondition::External` waits (per ft-ao9k9)
that hold for their full timeout: **the workflow can pin a pane
indefinitely**.

The pane lock is held for the workflow's full duration (per
runner.rs:367, lock_manager.try_acquire_with_limit). A pinned
pane means no other workflow can drive that pane.

## Why this is a real defect

Concrete failure scenarios:

1. **Wedged WaitCondition::External**: Per ft-ao9k9 fix, an
   external signal that NEVER fires hits the timeout. If the
   timeout is configured to a large value (e.g., 1h for a
   long-running mission), the pane is locked for an hour.

2. **Pathological retry loops.** A `Send` action whose
   verification step (the wait_for) consistently times out
   triggers retries; each retry burns the step budget; the
   workflow never makes forward progress but holds the lock.

3. **Operator can't cancel.** There's no documented `ft
   workflow stop <id>` that GUARANTEES a stuck workflow yields
   the lock within bounded time. A canceled Cx must propagate
   through every layer (per the modes-of-reasoning ft-k3y0u
   bead's drop-ordering work) — uncertain today.

The skill's domain audit for "workflows" surfaces this exactly:
it's the integration of step-timeout + retry-count + wait-
duration into a TOTAL runtime that's missing. Each component
has a bound; the composition doesn't.

## Suggested approach

1. **Add `total_timeout_ms` to `WorkflowRunnerConfig`**:

   ```rust
   pub struct WorkflowRunnerConfig {
       ...
       /// Maximum total runtime for any single workflow execution.
       /// Workflows running past this deadline are forcibly canceled
       /// AND the pane lock is released.
       pub total_timeout_ms: u64,   // suggested default: 600_000 (10 min)
   }
   ```

2. **Wire deadline propagation** through `execute_steps`:
   - Compute `deadline = start_time + total_timeout_ms` at
     workflow entry.
   - Each step gets a `step_deadline = min(step_timeout, deadline - now)`.
   - When deadline passes, return `WorkflowExecutionResult::TimedOut {
       execution_id, last_step, elapsed_ms
     }` and release the pane lock.

3. **Operator override**: per-workflow `total_timeout_ms` in the
   `Workflow` trait (each workflow declares its expected max
   runtime; runner enforces).

4. **Telemetry**: counter for
   `workflow.deadline_exceeded`, histogram for
   `workflow.execution_total_ms` so an operator can see
   pathological-runtime tail in `ft doctor`.

5. **Test**: `tests/workflow_total_deadline.rs` with a
   pathological workflow (always-timing-out external wait);
   assert deadline fires AND pane lock is released within the
   deadline + safety margin.

## Out of scope

- The `PaneLockGuard` refactor (separate bead ft-qkd2f covers
  the lock-leak invariant; this bead is about WHEN the
  guarded execution should be canceled).
- Workflow priority / preemption (different design surface).

## Acceptance criteria

- `WorkflowRunnerConfig::total_timeout_ms` field exists.
- The execution loop respects the deadline + emits a clean
  TimedOut result.
- The pane lock is released on deadline expiry.
- Regression test asserts a pathological 10-step workflow
  hits the deadline within tolerance and yields the lock.
- `ft doctor` exposes the `workflow.deadline_exceeded` counter.

## References

- `crates/frankenterm-core/src/workflows/runner.rs:218-237`
  (the config struct missing total_timeout)
- ft-ao9k9 (sibling: ExternalSignalRegistry wait — this is
  exactly the un-firing-external pathology)
- ft-qkd2f (sibling: PaneLockGuard refactor — same workflow
  layer, different invariant)
- ft-j0ufc (sibling: workflow privilege amplification —
  source-pane-trust separate from this deadline issue)

 {"id":"ft-3r0n4","title":"[profiling] wa.state at fleet scale: 200+ pane envelope serialization unbenched (mcp_response covers manifest only)","description":"## Hypothesis

`ft robot state` / `wa.state` is the highest-frequency robot call
in a swarm — every operator tick (every ~4 minutes per the
operator runbook) calls it once for fleet observability, plus
agent-driven calls.

The README sells frankenterm as "swarm-native" specifically for
**200+ concurrent AI coding agents**. The per-call cost of
`wa.state` at fleet scale (200 panes) directly drives the
operator-loop tail latency budget.

Existing benches:

- `benches/mcp_response.rs` — covers the **manifest list**
  serialization (tools[]) at varying tool counts, NOT the per-
  call response payload at varying pane counts.
- `benches/dashboard_aggregation.rs` — covers dashboard-side
  aggregation, not the robot-mode envelope production.

`grep -lE "wa.state|RobotStateResponse|render_robot_state"
benches/*.rs` returns zero hits.

## Why a profile + bench would deliver real perf gain

`wa.state` builds an envelope of `Vec<PaneInfo>` from the live
mux; serializes; redacts; renders TOON or JSON. At 200 panes:

- **Mux query cost** — how many RPCs to wezterm? One bulk vs
  one-per-pane?
- **Redaction cost** — secret patterns run against every pane's
  metadata; aho-corasick matcher passes per pane; bounded by
  pattern-pack size, but unmeasured.
- **Serialization cost** — JSON vs TOON delta at 200 panes.
- **Memory** — peak RSS during the envelope build; if the
  builder allocates a temporary `Vec<PaneInfo>` plus a JSON
  string + a TOON string, that's 3 copies of the same data.
- **Allocation hot path** — how many heap allocs for a 200-pane
  envelope? Each pane has nested HashMap<String, Value> in
  `extra` — those are small allocs that dominate.

Per the skill's scaling-law discipline: capture p95 ratio across
1, 10, 50, 100, 200 panes — if the curve is super-linear, there's
real attribution work to do.

## Suggested instrumentation + bench

1. **Bench**: `crates/frankenterm-core/benches/robot_state_at_scale.rs`
   - Construct a synthetic mux with 1, 10, 50, 100, 200, 500
     panes (deterministic PaneInfo generators).
   - Drive the same code path `wa.state` calls (likely
     `mcp_helpers::handle_state` or equivalent).
   - Measure: p50/p95/p99/p99.9 ms, ops/sec, peak RSS, alloc
     count per call (via dhat).
   - Output formats: JSON + TOON, both measured.

2. **Profile flame** at 200 panes for 30 seconds with samply.

3. **Hot-path attribution** — split:
   - Mux RPC (fastest)
   - PaneInfo construction
   - Serialization (JSON vs TOON — feeds into ft-0zoq3)
   - Redaction sweep

4. **Differential**: compare 200-pane wa.state to 200-pane
   `ft list` plain-text rendering (no MCP envelope) to isolate
   the envelope-build cost from the data-fetch cost.

## Hypothesis ledger entries

```
serialization-not-mux-dominates : pending — flame shows
                                   serde_json::to_string >>
                                   mux::list_panes
extra-hashmap-allocs-dominate   : pending — dhat shows
                                   HashMap<String, Value> per
                                   PaneInfo dominates count
toon-2x-slower-than-json-here   : pending — links to ft-0zoq3
                                   evidence
super-linear-scaling            : pending — p95 ratio at 200/1
                                   exceeds 250
peak-rss-3x-payload-size        : pending — RSS during build
                                   exceeds final envelope size
                                   by ≥3×
```

## Out of scope

- Mux RPC plumbing (`mux_client_ops.rs`,
  `mux_pool_scaling.rs` already cover it).
- The TOON encoder itself (sibling: ft-0zoq3 — TOON encoder
  bench).
- The dashboard rendering (`dashboard_aggregation.rs`).

## Acceptance criteria

- `benches/robot_state_at_scale.rs` exists, exercises 6 pane-
  count points × 2 output formats.
- Hotspot table at 200 panes attributes cost across the four
  hot-path stages.
- Hypothesis ledger entries marked with evidence paths.
- Profile artifact under
  `tests/artifacts/perf/wa-state-fleet-<run-id>/` with
  fingerprint.

## References

- `crates/frankenterm-core/src/mcp_helpers.rs` (likely
  `wa.state` handler)
- README §Tiered Scrollback ("designed for 200+ pane fleets")
- `docs/operator-runbook.md` §3 ("4-minute steady-state tick"
  cadence)
- Sibling bead: ft-0zoq3 (TOON encoder bench feeds the format
  comparison side)

Filed by cc_1 profiling audit, 2026-04-28.","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-04-28T09:46:42.635866Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-28T09:46:42.635866Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
 {"id":"ft-3se13","title":"[security-audit] policy decision log captures attacker-controlled text via PolicyInput.text_summary — secrets-in-audit leak","description":"## Concrete attack

`crates/frankenterm-core/src/policy.rs:4900` (the canonical
authorize entry):

```rust
pub fn authorize(&mut self, input: &PolicyInput) -> PolicyDecision {
    let decision = self.evaluate_authorization(input, None, None, true);
    self.record_to_decision_log(input, &decision);   // ← logs INPUT
    decision
}
```

`PolicyInput` carries a `text_summary` field that callers populate
with the action's payload. Per `mcp_tools.rs:1524`:

```rust
let summary = format!("wa.get_text pane_id={}", params.pane_id);
let mut input = mcp_get_text_policy_input(params.pane_id, ..., &summary);
```

That particular case is metadata-only. But other call sites pass
the actual TEXT being sent:

```rust
// mcp_tools.rs send-text path
input = input.with_text_summary(format!("send_text {} bytes", text.len()))
   .with_command_text(text.clone());   // ← whole user-typed text
```

`with_command_text(text.clone())` puts the FULL text into the
policy input. `record_to_decision_log` then writes it to the
decision log without going through `redact_secrets()`.

If a user (or the SOURCE agent) types a secret into a pane via
`ft robot send-text` or MCP `wa.send_text`, that secret is:

1. **NOT redacted on the way IN** — that's by design (you're
   intentionally sending it).
2. **Stored in the decision log unredacted** — because the
   decision log captures the ENTIRE PolicyInput including
   command_text, and the redactor isn't applied to the audit
   record before persistence.

Result: the audit/forensics surface accumulates secrets in
plaintext over time. An attacker with read access to the audit
log (legitimate operator? compromised support engineer?
forensic-export consumer?) gets every secret ever sent.

## Why this is exploitable

Per the skill's `⊕ Creative-Transposition` operator
(disgruntled-insider persona): an ex-employee with offline
copies of the audit log can mine years of operator typing for
credentials. Per Axiom 7 (recovery path is shadow codebase),
the audit log is a recovery surface that should re-enforce the
same redaction invariants as the live read surfaces — and
doesn't.

The `redactor.rs` test surface specifically covers
`AuditActionRecord::redact_fields` (storage.rs:2966) — which
proves the project's authors KNEW the audit surface needed
redaction. But the redactor is applied DOWNSTREAM at storage
write time on a different code path; the decision log captured
inside the policy engine doesn't go through that same redactor.

## Reproduction (concrete)

```bash
# Send a fake secret via MCP send-text (any matching prefix)
echo '{"pane_id":0,"text":"export OPENAI_API_KEY=sk-fake1234567890abcdef..."}' | \
  cargo run -p frankenterm -- mcp serve --stdio

# Inspect the decision log
sqlite3 ~/.local/share/ft/db.sqlite \
  "SELECT json_extract(input, '$.command_text') FROM policy_decision_log
   ORDER BY created_at DESC LIMIT 1"
# → "export OPENAI_API_KEY=sk-fake1234567890abcdef..."   (UNREDACTED)
```

## Suggested fix

1. **Apply redactor at decision-log write time.** Modify
   `record_to_decision_log` to redact `text_summary` and
   `command_text` before persisting:

   ```rust
   fn record_to_decision_log(&mut self, input: &PolicyInput, decision: &PolicyDecision) {
       let mut redacted_input = input.clone();
       if let Some(t) = redacted_input.text_summary.as_ref() {
           redacted_input.text_summary = Some(self.redact_secrets(t));
       }
       if let Some(c) = redacted_input.command_text.as_ref() {
           redacted_input.command_text = Some(self.redact_secrets(c));
       }
       // existing persistence logic, with redacted_input
   }
   ```

2. **Add a regression test**
   `tests/policy_decision_log_redacts_secrets.rs` that:
   - Constructs a `PolicyInput` with `command_text` containing
     each provider token family (xai-, sk-ant-, gh*_, AKIA, etc.).
   - Calls `authorize`.
   - Reads back from the decision-log table.
   - Asserts every column with text content is redacted.

3. **Document the contract** in
   `crates/frankenterm-core/src/policy.rs` doc comment: "the
   decision log redacts secrets before persistence; callers
   must NOT assume audit retains plaintext."

## Out of scope

- Field-level encryption at rest (orthogonal — separate bead if
  enterprises need it).
- Forensic-export redaction (`forensic_export.rs` has its own
  redaction pass — verify alignment in a sibling bead if
  needed).

## Acceptance criteria

- `record_to_decision_log` applies the redactor to text fields
  before persistence.
- Regression test asserts the matrix above (≥7 provider
  families) is fully redacted in the decision-log table.
- README §Safety Guarantees gains an explicit "audit logs are
  redacted in addition to live read surfaces."

## References

- `crates/frankenterm-core/src/policy.rs:4900-4906` (the
  authorize → record_to_decision_log call)
- `crates/frankenterm-core/src/policy.rs:5874` (engine.redact_secrets
  exists; not wired to the decision log)
- `crates/frankenterm-core/src/storage.rs:2966`
  (`AuditActionRecord::redact_fields` — the partner surface that
  IS wired)
- README §Safety Guarantees
- Skill: security-audit-for-saas Axiom 7 (recovery path is
  shadow codebase) + KEY-MANAGEMENT.md (PII never in error
  messages, logs, or stack traces)

 {"id":"ft-3tvvt","title":"[modes-of-reasoning][async-vs-sync] storage.rs has 6+ block_on calls — library secretly owns the runtime instead of borrowing from it","description":"## Perspective: async vs sync boundary

A library that calls `block_on(...)` is making a unilateral
decision about runtime ownership: *"I will start my own executor
to drive this Future to completion"*. That is binary behavior
disguised as library code.

Concrete evidence in `crates/frankenterm-core/src/storage.rs`:

- Line 12215: `crate::runtime_async::CompatRuntime::block_on(&runtime, rx.recv(&recv_cx)).ok();`
- Line 23543: `runtime.block_on(future);`
- Line 27947: `runtime.block_on(future);`
- Line 29831: `runtime.block_on(future);`
- Line 30090: `runtime.block_on(future);`
- Line 30460: `let verification_results: Vec<(u64, Vec<u64>)> = rt.block_on(async { ... });`

Six `block_on` call sites inside a library file. The README's
"Platform Model" section says the runtime model is
"asupersync-native in the product-facing APIs: structured,
cancel-correct, and centered on Cx-aware orchestration" — but
`block_on` inside library code is the OPPOSITE of cancel-correct
and Cx-aware: it captures the calling thread, ignores any
ambient `Cx`, and converts a structured async failure into a
sync deadlock if the caller is already on a runtime.

## Why this is a real architectural concern

Two cascading risks:

1. **Deadlock if a downstream crate is already on a runtime.**
   `frankenterm-gui` and `frankenterm-mux-server` are async
   binaries. If either calls a storage path that internally
   `block_on`s while ALREADY on the gui's wgpu/winit dispatcher
   thread, the runtime-within-runtime nesting deadlocks. Tokio
   panics on this; asupersync's behavior is documented in
   AGENTS.md but the risk is real.

2. **Cx cancellation is bypassed.** A `block_on` ignores the
   ambient cancellation context. If the caller's Cx is
   canceled, the storage operation continues to completion.
   This contradicts MEMORY.md's `asupersync-midflight-cancel-gap`
   note exactly: storage paths can block past a cancel signal.

The README explicitly markets cancel-correctness as a load-bearing
contract. Library code that internally `block_on`s breaks that
contract for any caller path it touches.

## Suggested approach

1. **Inventory the six call sites.** For each, classify:
   - Is this called from sync code only? (then OK at the binary
     boundary, but should still be moved OUT of the library)
   - Is this called from async code? (then it's a deadlock waiting
     to happen; convert to `.await`)
   - Is this in a `Drop` impl? (acceptable per
     `cancellation_safe_channel` patterns, but document why)

2. **Forbid new `block_on` inside `frankenterm-core`** via a
   `#[clippy::disallowed_methods]` lint or a custom check in
   `scripts/check_no_runtime_regression.sh` (which already
   guards similar patterns).

3. **For the async-call-site cases**: convert to `pub async fn`
   and let the binary decide.

4. **For the sync-call-site cases**: extract the sync wrapper
   to the binary crate (`crates/frankenterm/src/main.rs` or a
   new `crates/frankenterm-cli-storage` shim).

5. **Add a regression test** that asserts `frankenterm-core`
   compiles + tests pass with `block_on` mechanically removed,
   so future drift surfaces in CI.

## Out of scope

- `runtime_async::CompatRuntime::block_on` itself (it is the
  documented compat shim per AGENTS.md / ft-xbnl0; this bead
  removes its callers from library code, not the shim).
- Drop-impl cleanup paths that legitimately need to drain a
  channel synchronously (those are the documented `cancellation_safe_channel`
  pattern; flag them with a comment instead of removing).

## Acceptance criteria

- Zero `block_on(` matches under `crates/frankenterm-core/src/`
  except in `runtime_async.rs`, `runtime.rs`, and any documented
  exception list.
- `scripts/check_no_runtime_regression.sh` is extended to gate
  this in CI.
- The six current call sites are individually addressed in
  follow-on PRs (one per site, with tests proving the new
  async-or-binary placement preserves behavior).

## References

- `crates/frankenterm-core/src/storage.rs:12215, 23543, 27947, 29831, 30090, 30460`
- README §Platform Model (cancel-correctness contract)
- MEMORY.md `asupersync-midflight-cancel-gap` (the related
  pre-cancel/mid-flight asymmetry)
- `scripts/check_no_runtime_regression.sh` (existing CI guard
  pattern to extend)
- ft-xbnl0 epic (the broader runtime-collapse tracking)

 {"id":"ft-5490a","title":"[golden-artifacts] TOON encoder: format=toon emits across robot+MCP, no exact-bytes corpus pinning the rendered output","description":"## The gap

ft's robot mode and MCP layer support `format=toon` for token-
efficient AI-to-AI output (per README's TL;DR: 'TOON-compact —
use this when piping to an AI'). Two existing tests:

- `tests/proptest_toon_roundtrip.rs` (21 KB) — proves
  `serialize → deserialize == identity` for arbitrary inputs.
- `tests/wa_mission_toon_mcp_conformance.rs` and
  `wa_tx_toon_mcp_conformance.rs` — assert TOON output for specific
  mission/tx envelopes parses back into the expected JSON shape.
- `tests/golden_pane_state_toon.rs` — pins ONE TOON output (pane
  state). 5.6 KB.

Property tests enforce shape invariants. The mission/tx tests
enforce parses-back-to-JSON. **Neither pins the exact rendered
TOON bytes for the broader robot envelope corpus.**

`grep -lE "toon.*golden\|TOON_GOLDEN\|to_toon.*golden"
tests/*.rs` returns zero hits beyond `golden_pane_state_toon.rs`.

## Why this catches real defects (skill criterion)

The README's TL;DR sells TOON specifically as 'token-efficient AI-
to-AI output'. The encoded *bytes* are the value proposition — a
new field on a robot envelope that adds 50 tokens per response,
or a re-ordering that makes the output less compressible by an
AI's vocabulary, breaks the entire 'token-efficient' contract
silently. Property tests don't catch this because they preserve
roundtripability, not byte-economy.

Concrete drift hazards a corpus would catch:
- TOON encoder version change reorders fields, blowing up token
  counts.
- A robot tool gains an `Option<String>` field that serializes as
  `key=null,` even when None — invisible in JSON, present in TOON,
  uses tokens.
- Whitespace drift between major TOON versions silently hits the
  full robot/MCP surface.

## Suggested implementation

Mirror the structure of `tests/golden_robot_envelope/` (which I
shipped runtime-validation for in ft-5ikbd) but for TOON output:

1. **Corpus** at `tests/fixtures/toon_envelope_corpus/<tool>.toon`
   with one frozen TOON rendering per robot tool that pins
   meaningful output (state, get-text, search, send, mission_state,
   tx_run, tx_show, …).
2. **Generator** (or test-side fixture builder) that produces a
   deterministic input envelope per fixture and renders it via the
   production TOON encoder. The same input is checked into
   `tests/fixtures/toon_envelope_corpus/<tool>.input.json` so the
   regeneration command is auditable.
3. **Test driver**
   `tests/conformance_toon_envelope_corpus.rs`:
   - For each `<tool>.input.json`, render via the production TOON
     encoder, scrub timestamps/elapsed_ms/durations, diff against
     `<tool>.toon`.
   - Bonus: assert byte-count is within ±10% of a stored
     `<tool>.byte_budget.txt` so regressions in token-economy
     surface as test failures (not just silent bloat).
4. **UPDATE_GOLDENS workflow** — same pattern as the existing
   `tests/golden/` infrastructure (PROVENANCE.md row, diff review).

## Out of scope

- Comparing TOON to JSON byte counts (token economy is the focus,
  but cross-format comparison is a separate analysis).
- Reranking / weighting of which tools are highest-value to pin
  (start with the README-enumerated robot family).

## Acceptance criteria

- ≥10 `<tool>.toon` goldens across the robot envelope family.
- A new field on a robot tool's response struct that serializes
  as TOON makes the corresponding golden fail with a precise diff.
- Byte-budget assertion (or equivalent) flags >10% regression.
- PROVENANCE.md row records the encoder version pinned at
  generation.

## References

- `tests/proptest_toon_roundtrip.rs` (existing property coverage)
- `tests/golden_pane_state_toon.rs` (the existing single TOON
  golden — template for the corpus expansion)
- README §TL;DR (TOON's stated value proposition)
- ft-5ikbd (sibling: JSON envelope schema validator that established
  the corpus directory pattern)

 {"id":"ft-55l4f","title":"[review][MEDIUM] wa_wait_for.json golden freezes polls:1 — may flake under high-concurrency CI load","description":"In crates/frankenterm-core/tests/golden_robot_envelope/wa_wait_for.json (landed in ecae6caa):

    "polls": 1

The canonicalize fn in crates/frankenterm-core/tests/mcp_conformance_core_tools.rs only zeroes `now | elapsed_ms | captured_at | *_ms | score | semantic_score`. The `polls` field is not canonicalized.

Under load, this can flake:
- Each wa.wait_for poll spawns a fresh python3 subprocess (fake-wezterm.py) via tokio::Command in crates/frankenterm-core/src/wezterm.rs:2142-2155.
- On first-poll cache-cold with the swarm running 20+ agents hammering CPU/disk, the subprocess spawn + file read can be slow enough that the poll scheduler issues a second poll before the first returns matching text (build ready matches ready$ immediately on content).
- polls jumps to 2+, golden is polls==1, test fails.

Repro likelihood: low under bench, moderate-to-high under concurrent cargo test + rch + other agent builds hammering I/O.

Fix options (pick one):
1. Canonicalize `polls` to 0 in canonicalize() alongside elapsed_ms/now/captured_at. Pro: stable golden. Con: loses a genuine signal — golden no longer catches regressions where wait_for suddenly needs 10 polls for a match it used to catch on the first try.
2. Split `polls` out of the golden capture: keep it in success_envelope but canonicalize to "polls >= 1" assertion in assert_wait_for_success_data (already there at mcp_conformance_core_tools.rs:~590 — `polls >= 1`), then canonicalize polls to 0 so the golden only sees the structural presence.
3. Accept the flake risk and document that re-running tests clears transient failures.

Option 2 is cleanest: the semantic "polls >= 1" invariant stays enforced; the byte-stable golden covers structure.

Severity: MEDIUM. Not a correctness bug; CI flake risk. Discovered during reviewer audit round 7, 2026-04-23 when verifying ecae6caa (ft-qyw1z+ft-4o5mb CRITICAL fix).

+{"id":"ft-5cl1b","title":"[codebase-audit][fleet/replay] cross-architecture replay determinism unverified — README markets 'deterministic replay' across heterogeneous fleets","description":"## Domain gap

The README §Replay & Forensics section markets:

> Capture, replay, and diff decision graphs for post-incident
> analysis and regression testing.

The "Decision replay | Normalized DAG + causal edges + diff
engine" claim in the Key Algorithms table implies replay
across hosts. The `tests/replay_decision_diff_golden.rs` (26 KB)
covers same-host determinism. But concrete **cross-architecture
determinism** (e.g., capture on aarch64-apple-darwin, replay on
x86_64-unknown-linux-gnu) is unverified.

## What's missing (not caught by prior audits)

Sources of cross-architecture nondeterminism that none of the
prior audits surfaced:

1. **Floating-point ordering.** Any `Vec<f64>::sort_by` or
   reduction that's not bit-identical across compiler/CPU
   variants. The `recorder_query.rs` ranking, the
   `hybrid_search.rs` RRF fusion, the `bocpd.rs` change-point
   detector — all candidates.

2. **HashMap iteration order.** Default `HashMap` iteration
   order depends on RandomState seed → SipHash with an arch-
   specific entropy pool. Replay events are PRESERVED in
   serialized form, but **derived structures** (HashMap-backed
   indexes, `extra` field iteration) diverge across hosts.

3. **`time::Instant` resolution.** macOS uses
   `mach_continuous_time()` (1ns); Linux uses `CLOCK_MONOTONIC`
   (typically 1µs). Replay timestamps may quantize differently;
   any duration arithmetic that depends on tick boundaries
   diverges.

4. **`std::process::id()`, `std::thread::current().id()`.**
   Embedded in error messages, telemetry samples, replay
   captures. Tests pin a workspace-id but live process IDs
   leak through.

5. **Unicode width tables.** `unicode-width` crate version
   pinning is per-Cargo.lock, but the library's Unicode data
   table version may differ if bundled separately.

6. **`zstd` compression nondeterminism.** `byte_compression.rs`
   compresses pane output. zstd is deterministic on a single
   version + level + dictionary, but version drift across
   hosts breaks byte-identical comparison of compressed
   payloads.

## Why this is a real defect

The README's "post-incident analysis" use case is precisely the
cross-host case: an incident happens on host A; the operator
exports the snapshot; replay runs on host B (the engineer's
workstation). If replay diverges, the engineer can't reproduce
the failure. The marketing claim is then false.

The skill's `codebase-audit` lens specifically catches "marketing
claim with no test gate" — every prior audit examined ft on a
single host with single configuration; the cross-host gradient
is structurally invisible from inside any single-host test.

## Suggested approach

1. **Differential CI matrix**:
   - Capture a synthetic 100-event session on macos-14.
   - Upload the capture file as an artifact.
   - On ubuntu-latest, download the artifact and run
     `ft session replay <file>`.
   - Diff: pane state at each step, decision graph nodes,
     emitted events. Assert byte-identical OR document the
     accepted divergence classes (timestamps, process IDs).

2. **Determinism contract document** at
   `docs/replay-determinism-contract.md`:
   - Enumerate accepted nondeterminism (timestamps, PIDs,
     hostnames).
   - Enumerate must-be-deterministic (event ordering, decision
     edges, payload bytes).
   - For each "accepted" item, name the canonical scrubber.

3. **Scrubber library** for replay artifacts:
   `crates/frankenterm-core-replay/src/scrubbers.rs` with one
   scrubber per nondeterminism source (UUID, timestamp,
   process_id, etc.). Mirrors the
   `testing-golden-artifacts` skill's scrubber pattern.

4. **Replace any floating-point hot paths with integer
   arithmetic where determinism matters** (RRF fusion,
   change-point detection). If float is unavoidable, document
   the bit-identity expectation per platform.

5. **Test**: `tests/cross_arch_replay_determinism.rs` that
   simulates two architectures (via different `RandomState`
   seeds and time-quantum mocks) and asserts replay yields
   identical post-scrubbing output.

## Out of scope

- The `byte_compression.rs` zstd version pinning (orthogonal
  — pin via Cargo.lock).
- The full GUI replay UX (wezterm-side determinism is its own
  audit).

## Acceptance criteria

- `docs/replay-determinism-contract.md` exists with the
  accepted/required-determinism matrix.
- A CI job captures on macos-14 and replays on ubuntu-latest;
  passes byte-identical OR scrubbed-equal.
- The scrubber library covers every accepted-nondeterminism
  source.
- Regression test exercises two-architecture-mock determinism.

## References

- README §Replay & Forensics ("Decision replay | Normalized DAG")
- `crates/frankenterm-core/tests/replay_decision_diff_golden.rs`
  (single-host determinism only)
- `docs/proposals/ft-j1qjt-replay-tier1-blocker.md` (related
  replay work)
- Skill: codebase-audit — "domain coverage matrix" (cross-arch
  is the one axis no single-host test exercises)
- Skill: testing-golden-artifacts §Pattern 5 (Canonicalized
  Golden) — direct precedent for the scrubber library

 {"id":"ft-5ikbd","title":"[conformance] robot-mode JSON envelopes: no runtime validator against docs/json-schema/","description":"## The gap

`docs/mcp-api-spec.md` states (verbatim):

> `data` MUST match the corresponding robot JSON schema under
> `docs/json-schema/`.

This is the central MUST clause of the MCP/robot surface. There is no
runtime test that enforces it.

## What exists today

- `crates/frankenterm-core/tests/schema_golden.rs` validates that:
  - Each `docs/json-schema/wa-robot-*.json` is well-formed JSON
  - Each registry entry maps to a file on disk
  - Each file has the required JSON-Schema metadata fields
  - `docs_gen` is deterministic
- `crates/frankenterm-core/tests/golden_robot_envelope/*.json` pins
  individual envelope *examples* but does not validate envelopes
  against the schema — it compares serialized output to a hand-crafted
  fixture.

## What's missing

- No `jsonschema` (or equivalent) crate in `Cargo.toml` workspace deps.
  `rg "jsonschema = " Cargo.toml crates/*/Cargo.toml` returns nothing.
- No test that takes a real `RobotResponse` / MCP tool envelope, runs
  it through `serde_json::to_value`, and validates against the
  corresponding `wa-robot-*.json` schema with a real Draft validator.
- The `additional_properties_leak` test in `schema_golden.rs` only
  checks the schemas themselves; a Rust type that adds a field but
  forgets to update the schema would land green.

## Why this catches real defects (skill criterion)

A `Robot*Response` struct gains a new `Option<u64>` field. The struct
serializes fine. `golden_robot_envelope/wa_state.json` tested a
specific case that doesn't exercise the new field, so it stays green.
`docs/json-schema/wa-robot-state.json` does NOT have the new field.
**Downstream JSON consumers (TypeScript clients, MCP agents) break.**
A schema-conformance harness catches this on the same commit that
adds the field.

This is exactly the differential / golden-file pattern the conformance
skill (Pattern 3) prescribes for serialization formats with an
authoritative spec.

## Suggested implementation

1. Add `jsonschema = "0.18"` (or equivalent) to dev-dependencies.
2. New test file `tests/conformance_robot_envelope_schema.rs`:
   - For each `(endpoint, schema_file)` pair from `SchemaRegistry`:
     - Deserialize the corresponding `golden_robot_envelope/*.json`
       fixture into the typed `Robot*Response`.
     - Re-serialize via `serde_json::to_value`.
     - Validate the serialized `Value` against the schema.
   - For each top-level production envelope returned by mcp_helpers,
     run a synthetic happy-path producer + validator.
3. Bonus: a coverage-matrix table at the bottom of the test that
   prints `(schema, validations_run, validations_passing)` so we can
   read it in CI and check that every shipped schema is reached.

## Out of scope

- Generating schemas FROM Rust types (that's `api_schema::SchemaRegistry`'s
  existing job; this bead validates the OTHER direction).
- Validating non-robot/MCP JSON outputs (separate beads if needed).

## Acceptance criteria

- `cargo test -p frankenterm-core --test conformance_robot_envelope_schema`
  passes locally and in CI.
- Adding a field to a `Robot*Response` struct without updating the
  schema makes the test fail with a clear, fixture-tied error message.
- Test prints a coverage table: schemas exercised vs schemas in registry.

## References

- `docs/mcp-api-spec.md` (the MUST clause)
- `docs/json-schema/wa-robot-*.json` (the schemas)
- `crates/frankenterm-core/tests/schema_golden.rs` (existing well-formedness gate)
- `crates/frankenterm-core/tests/golden_robot_envelope/` (existing example fixtures)

 {"id":"ft-7s0vw","title":"event_id::detect_clock_anomaly overflows on prev + future_skew_threshold","description":"## Defect

\`crates/frankenterm-core/src/event_id.rs:205\` computes
\`prev + future_skew_threshold\` without overflow protection. In release
builds u64 wraps silently; in debug builds the unchecked add panics.
Either way, a pathological or adversarial \`prev\` value breaks the
future-skew branch.

## Concrete failure path

1. Caller invokes \`detect_clock_anomaly(current, prev, threshold)\` where
   \`prev + threshold\` overflows \`u64\`.
2. Easy reach: replay/import flows feeding \`recorded_at_ms = u64::MAX\` from
   a corrupt fixture, fuzzer, or attacker-controlled input. Also reachable
   via legitimate-but-distant clock sources (UNIX epoch micros around year
   586,524 if anyone reaches that, or any monotonic clock that has been
   bumped by a process bug into the high u64 range).
3. **Release build:** \`prev + threshold\` wraps to a tiny value. The
   condition \`current > tiny_value\` is now true for almost any \`current\`,
   so the function returns \`is_anomaly: true\` for every subsequent valid
   timestamp — flooding telemetry with false-positive "future skew"
   reports and potentially triggering downstream remediation that should
   not have fired.
4. **Debug build:** unchecked add panics (\`attempt to add with overflow\`),
   killing whatever thread was processing the recorder pipeline.
5. \`ClockAnomalyTracker::observe\` always updates the baseline
   ("Updates the baseline regardless") — so a single malicious or
   transiently-high \`current\` value sticks around and continues to
   poison overflow checks against subsequent observations.

## Severity rationale

- Silent wrong answer in release; thread-killing panic in debug.
- Adversary-reachable via fixture/replay/fuzz inputs.
- Same arithmetic-poison family as ft-761tz (disk_pressure NaN) and
  ft-yskcu (continuous_backpressure NaN): a hostile or pathological
  numeric input cascades into permanently-wrong tier/anomaly outputs
  with no self-recovery.

## Fix sketch

1. Replace \`prev + future_skew_threshold\` with \`prev.checked_add(threshold)\`.
2. On overflow, treat as "no future-skew anomaly" — we cannot prove a
   timestamp is too far in the future relative to a near-MAX baseline,
   so the only sound default is \"don't flag\". Same disposition as
   ft-761tz: when the math itself becomes unreliable, fail toward
   silence rather than toward false positives.
3. Regression tests covering: (a) overflow with threshold=1 and
   prev=u64::MAX → no anomaly, no panic; (b) overflow with threshold=
   u64::MAX and small prev → no anomaly, no panic; (c) the existing
   future-skew path still fires when math is in range.

## Scope

\`crates/frankenterm-core/src/event_id.rs\` only. No cross-crate API
changes. \`ClockAnomalyTracker::observe\` baseline-update behavior is
intentional and out of scope.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","assignee":"pane0","created_at":"2026-04-22T11:30:32.110899Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-22T11:36:43.255991Z","closed_at":"2026-04-22T11:36:43.255609Z","close_reason":"Fixed in commit 4431483f: detect_clock_anomaly now uses prev.checked_add(future_skew_threshold), failing silent on overflow rather than panicking (debug) or wrapping (release). 5 regression tests added covering (current=prev=u64::MAX, threshold=1), (huge threshold), (boundary u64::MAX × u64::MAX), (in-range path still fires), and (tracker survives MAX baseline). All 53 event_id tests pass against /tmp/ft-rv-p0-target. Pattern matches the arithmetic-poison family alongside ft-761tz (NaN) and ft-yskcu (NaN cascade).","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
 {"id":"ft-7tq4z","title":"[HIGH] SCHEMA_SQL drift: fresh DBs skip v24 policy_denied_audit table (storage.rs:4440-4446 sets user_version=SCHEMA_VERSION without running migrations)","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-26T01:52:09.392483Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-26T02:16:44.933511Z","closed_at":"2026-04-26T02:16:44.933247Z","close_reason":"92cd7452 fixes the SCHEMA_SQL drift by collapsing the two current==0 branches in initialize_schema into a single path through run_v0_init_in_transaction. The truly-fresh DB case now goes through repair (no-op when no tables exist) → SCHEMA_SQL → run_migrations(0) → ensure_ft_meta — same atomicity contract as ft-k542h/c06b230a. The buggy early-return that ran SCHEMA_SQL and stamped user_version=SCHEMA_VERSION directly (skipping run_migrations entirely) is deleted; needs_init is preserved for other callers (status reporters/diagnostics). Three test changes: (1) NEW fresh_db_init_creates_policy_denied_audit_via_v24_migration pins the regression — fresh DB has user_version==SCHEMA_VERSION AND the v24 policy_denied_audit table with all documented columns AND a record_policy_denial_audit_sync insert succeeds; (2) UPDATED schema_version_audit_trail_recorded asserts the new invariant (count>=1, max(version)==SCHEMA_VERSION, every version in 1..=SCHEMA_VERSION with non-null applied_at) instead of the old hardcoded count==1 / 'Initial schema'; (3) UPDATED idempotent_init_preserves_version compares snapshots (count_after_first==count_after_second) instead of hardcoding 1 — robust against MIGRATIONS growing. CLEANUP: ft-cro2u's wait_for_tool_persists test no longer needs the v24 CREATE TABLE workaround. 188 storage::tests pass via rch on /tmp/ft-cc6-target (3 unrelated recorder_storage frankensqlite gate failures pre-exist this commit).","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["bug","schema","storage"],"comments":[{"id":3641,"issue_id":"ft-7tq4z","author":"jemanuel","text":"# Discovered while writing ft-cro2u

Pre-existing bug surfaced by the new audit-persist test in ft-cro2u.
The fresh-DB initialization path at
`crates/frankenterm-core/src/storage.rs:4440-4446` is:

    if current == 0 && needs_init {
        conn.execute_batch(SCHEMA_SQL)?;
        set_user_version(conn, SCHEMA_VERSION)?;
        record_migration(conn, SCHEMA_VERSION, "Initial schema")?;
        ensure_ft_meta(conn, SCHEMA_VERSION)?;
        return Ok(());
    }

It runs the static `SCHEMA_SQL` blob and stamps the user_version
directly to `SCHEMA_VERSION` (currently 24) — but `SCHEMA_SQL` does
NOT include the `policy_denied_audit` table that landed in migration
v24. New databases never get that table created.

# Repro

A brand-new SQLite file opened via `StorageHandle::new(path)` has
`user_version = 24` (per `SCHEMA_VERSION`) but
`SELECT * FROM policy_denied_audit` returns
`SqliteFailure: no such table: policy_denied_audit`.

# Impact

* Every MCP policy-deny on a fresh DB silently fails the audit-persist
  step (`persist_mcp_policy_denial_async` warn-logs and continues).
  Operators get a denied response in tracing but no row in
  `policy_denied_audit` to query later.
* This affects new installs / new test harnesses / fresh CI workspaces.
  Existing user DBs that walked through migrations (v0 → v1 → … → v24)
  DID get the table because v24's `up_sql` ran for them.

# Two fixes

1. **Append CREATE TABLE policy_denied_audit (and its two indexes) to
   SCHEMA_SQL.** Each `CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS` is idempotent so
   existing migrated DBs won't double-create. Cheap. Makes fresh DBs
   match the migrated-up DBs.
2. **Run migrations on the fresh-DB path.** Replace the early-return
   with `conn.execute_batch(SCHEMA_SQL)?; set_user_version(conn, 0)?;
   run_migrations(conn, 0)?;` so every migration helper applies. More
   thorough but also runs every helper for tables SCHEMA_SQL already
   created — harmless idempotently, but extra work.

(1) is the smaller change and matches the documented invariant that
SCHEMA_SQL == "the canonical schema at the latest version". Fix (2)
would be the more general structural answer.

# Workaround in test scope (ft-cro2u)

The new `wait_for_tool_persists_policy_denial_audit_when_storage_is_attached`
test pre-seeds the table with a direct rusqlite connection before
invoking the tool, with an inline comment pointing at this bead. When
this bead lands the workaround block can be deleted.

# Out of scope

Other v24+ migrations should also be audited for SCHEMA_SQL drift —
this might not be the only orphan.","created_at":"2026-04-26T01:52:32Z"}]}
+{"id":"ft-7v53r","title":"[codebase-audit][mux/term/codec] mux pool socket-disappearance fault injection: no test exercises mid-RPC socket loss","description":"## Domain gap

The `frankenterm/mux` vendored crate plus `crates/frankenterm-core/src/wezterm.rs`
maintain a connection pool to the live wezterm mux socket
(`~/.local/share/wezterm/default.sock` per the README's vendored
config). Existing benches:

- `benches/mux_client_ops.rs` — happy-path RPC throughput
- `benches/mux_pool_scaling.rs` — pool size under load
- `tests/conformance_pdu_wire.rs` — codec frame correctness

What's missing: **fault injection where the underlying socket
disappears mid-operation**. No test exercises:

- Socket file deleted while a connection is held.
- mux server killed (SIGKILL) mid-RPC.
- Socket file replaced (atomic rename) mid-RPC.
- File-system unmounted (e.g., USB_NVME volume disappearing per
  the AGENTS.md note).

## Why this is a real defect

The README markets ft as a swarm-native control plane. Operators
hot-swap mux servers all the time — wezterm itself crashes,
Docker volumes detach, the socket gets `rm`'d during cleanup.
Every one of these is a "socket disappears mid-RPC" event.

Concrete failure modes that no current test catches:

1. **Stuck pool entries.** If the connection pool holds a
   broken connection and a caller `acquire()`s it, the next RPC
   blocks until OS-level timeout (TCP-style hang on a Unix
   socket — minutes, not seconds).

2. **Pool exhaustion via stuck entries.** If broken connections
   accumulate without eviction, the pool's `max_connections`
   quota fills with dead entries. New callers wait forever.

3. **Cancellation propagation.** A Cx canceled while waiting on
   a broken socket — does asupersync's cancellation actually
   wake the syscall? Per MEMORY.md's
   `asupersync-midflight-cancel-gap`, the answer is "not always."

The skill's domain-audit lens specifically catches this: every
prior audit (fuzzing, profiling, security, conformance, etc.)
focused on the input data flowing through the mux. None
exercised the mux as a faulted dependency.

## Suggested approach

1. **Fault-injection test harness** at
   `crates/frankenterm-core/tests/mux_socket_fault_injection.rs`:
   - Spawn a stub mux server bound to a temp Unix socket.
   - Start a steady-state RPC loop from the production
     `WeztermClient` (real code path, not mock).
   - Under `tokio::time::pause` (or asupersync equivalent),
     inject:
       a. Server `kill -9` mid-RPC.
       b. `unlink(socket_path)` while pool holds connections.
       c. `rename(socket_path, new_path)` (atomic swap).
   - Assert: every in-flight RPC returns `Err` with a
     specific error variant (not hang, not panic).
   - Assert: subsequent `acquire()` calls succeed against a
     restarted server within N seconds (pool self-heals).

2. **Add a `mux_pool.health_check_ms` config knob** for periodic
   liveness pings; broken connections evicted before the next
   acquire.

3. **Cancellation-correctness test**: spawn a long blocking
   RPC, cancel the Cx, assert the await wakes within a bounded
   deadline.

4. **Telemetry**: counter for `mux.socket_lost_events`,
   `mux.connection_evictions`, `mux.acquire_wait_p95`. Surface
   in `ft doctor`.

## Out of scope

- Implementing automatic mux-server restart (operator concern).
- Cross-host transport (distributed mode is its own surface;
  filed earlier as ft-h8v8v fuzz target for WireEnvelope).

## Acceptance criteria

- `tests/mux_socket_fault_injection.rs` exists with at least
  three injection scenarios.
- Each test asserts bounded recovery time (< 5s) and no
  panic/hang.
- Cancellation-correctness test covers the
  asupersync-midflight-cancel path.
- Telemetry counters land + appear in `ft doctor` output.

## References

- `crates/frankenterm-core/src/wezterm.rs` (pool definition,
  mux RPC entry point)
- `frankenterm/mux/` (vendored mux crate)
- README §Vendored config (`mux_socket_path`)
- MEMORY.md `asupersync-midflight-cancel-gap`

+{"id":"ft-ctt7k","title":"[codebase-audit][capture/storage] WAL checkpoint cost under sustained-write load is unmeasured + ungoverned","description":"## Domain gap

`crates/frankenterm-core/src/storage.rs` opens SQLite in WAL mode
(per the existing benches and `recorder_storage.rs` — confirmed
by `PRAGMA journal_mode=WAL` patterns in the schema setup). WAL
mode trades disk-write durability for read concurrency: writes
go to a sidecar `.db-wal` file; checkpoints fold the WAL back
into the main DB on a periodic schedule (default: when the WAL
hits ~4MB).

## What's missing (not caught by prior audits)

- `benches/recorder_ingest.rs` (proposed in ft-9r82k) covers
  ingest CPU/serialize cost.
- `tests/storage_regression.rs` (34 KB) covers schema regression.
- 9 migration tests + proptest_recorder.* cover migration safety.

**None measure or govern WAL checkpoint cost.** Specifically:

1. **Checkpoint stalls under sustained writes.** SQLite's auto-
   checkpoint runs synchronously on the writer thread when the
   WAL crosses the threshold. At fleet scale (200 panes × 10
   events/sec = 2000 events/sec), the WAL fills in seconds; the
   checkpoint stalls every writer for the duration of the fold.
   p99 latency on the recorder hot path likely has an unbounded
   tail driven by checkpoint cadence.

2. **WAL grows unbounded if checkpoint blocks.** A single
   long-running read query holds the WAL "in use" — auto-
   checkpoint fails, WAL grows past the `journal_size_limit`
   pragma if not configured, and disk fills. ft has no
   explicit configuration of `wal_autocheckpoint`,
   `journal_size_limit`, or `synchronous` PRAGMAs.

3. **No adaptive throttle.** When checkpoints are running long
   (because of blocking readers or just disk pressure), the
   ingest path doesn't know to slow down. Per the README's
   backpressure hierarchy (Green/Yellow/Red/Black), there's
   no checkpoint-cost-aware tier.

This is exactly the SaaS pattern of "fail-open under sustained
load" — but for a local SQLite store rather than Redis.

## Why this catches real defects

Concrete failure scenarios:

- **Operator runs a swarm session for 8 hours.** The WAL
  accumulates because of a long-running `ft search` that
  pinned readers across a checkpoint. Disk fills overnight.
  Recorder writes start failing.
- **`ft session restore` reads from a 100 MB WAL.** Restore
  takes minutes because every read joins the main DB + WAL.
- **Tail latency on the recorder hot path** spikes every 30s
  as auto-checkpoint runs. Per the profiling skill's tail-
  latency discipline, p99 must be measured — today p50 is fine
  but p99 hides a checkpoint stall.

## Suggested approach

1. **Bench**: `benches/wal_checkpoint_cost.rs` —
   - Sustained-write workload (10K events/sec for 60s).
   - Measure: WAL size over time, checkpoint frequency,
     per-event p50/p95/p99/p99.9 latency.
   - Compare three PRAGMA configurations: default,
     `synchronous=NORMAL+wal_autocheckpoint=10000`, manual
     checkpoint via `wal_checkpoint(TRUNCATE)`.

2. **Configuration knobs in ft.toml**:
   ```toml
   [storage.wal]
   autocheckpoint_pages = 10000
   synchronous = "normal"   # or "full" / "off"
   journal_size_limit_mb = 64
   ```

3. **Telemetry counter** for checkpoint events:
   `counter!("storage.wal_checkpoint_ms")`. The diagnostic
   surface (per ft-40k1o's ft doctor goldens) shows the
   distribution.

4. **Backpressure integration**: when WAL size > threshold OR
   recent checkpoint took > N ms, raise to Yellow tier so
   ingest knows to throttle.

## Out of scope

- Migrating to a different storage engine (orthogonal — WAL is
  fine; the audit gap is the lack of measurement).
- The `frankensqlite` rollout-only backend (per README hedge).

## Acceptance criteria

- `benches/wal_checkpoint_cost.rs` exists and runs the three
  PRAGMA configurations.
- p99 latency under sustained 10K-events/sec load is documented
  in `docs/perf-ledger/wal-checkpoint.md`.
- `[storage.wal]` config block is wired to the production loader.
- Telemetry counter for checkpoint duration lands.
- Per the profiling skill's hand-off contract, this bead stops
  at the hotspot table; optimization (if needed) becomes a
  follow-on bead per `extreme-software-optimization` skill.

## References

- `crates/frankenterm-core/src/storage.rs` (WAL mode setup)
- ft-9r82k (sibling: recorder ingest bench — covers
  serialize/flush, NOT WAL checkpoint specifically)
- README §Replay & Forensics
- Skill: profiling-software-performance — IO-AND-TRADEOFFS.md
  ("disk is the bottleneck" trigger; biolatency for fsync)

+{"id":"ft-pe4ds","title":"[codebase-audit][robot/MCP] error-envelope schema is unvalidated — runtime gate (ft-5ikbd) only covers success_envelope","description":"## Domain gap

ft-5ikbd (commit 04911fff7) shipped the runtime jsonschema validator
for robot envelopes. The fixture loader at
`tests/conformance_robot_envelope_schema.rs:116` extracts the
**`success_envelope`** field from each `wa_*.json` golden.

The MCP/robot envelope schema (`docs/json-schema/wa-robot-envelope.json`)
covers BOTH success and failure shapes via the `if/then` clause:

```json
"if": { "properties": { "ok": { "const": true } } },
"then": { "required": ["data"] }
```

…and the schema separately requires `error` + `error_code`
fields with a `^robot\\.[a-z_]+$` pattern when `ok=false`.

**The runtime validator never exercises the `ok=false` branch
against real fixtures.** The `wa_*.json` fixtures only carry a
`success_envelope` field. Error envelopes ARE produced in
production (`MCP_ERR_PANE_NOT_FOUND`, `MCP_ERR_POLICY`,
`MCP_ERR_INVALID_ARGS` per `mcp_tools.rs`), but no golden pins
their shape and no test validates them.

## Why this is a real defect

Concrete failure scenarios:

1. **A new error variant ships with a malformed `error_code`.**
   The schema requires `^robot\\.[a-z_]+$`. A code like
   `robot.not-implemented` (hyphen) or `robot.NotFound` (caps)
   would slip through today because no test runs an error
   envelope through the validator.

2. **A `data: null` leak.** Per the schema's `if/then`,
   when `ok=true`, `data` MUST be present. The complement —
   when `ok=false`, the `data` field is OPTIONAL but if
   present it must be parseable. A handler that returns
   `ok=false` PLUS a malformed `data` slips by.

3. **Hint string contains a secret.** The `hint` field is
   free-form. ft-3se13 (security audit) catches this for
   the decision log; the same risk exists at the response
   surface but no current test asserts the hint goes through
   redaction.

ft-5ikbd's three falsification gates
(`missing-required-field`, `malformed-error-code`,
`ok-true-without-data`) prove the validator works on
SYNTHETIC error envelopes — but no REAL production error
path is tested.

## Suggested approach

1. **Extend the fixture corpus** at
   `tests/golden_robot_envelope/`:
   - Add a sibling `error_envelope` field to each `wa_*.json`
     fixture that has a known error path (e.g., `wa_state.json`
     with the "wezterm not running" error).
   - Or: add new `wa_*_error.json` fixtures that pin specific
     error variants (one per error_code constant in
     `mcp_tools.rs`).

2. **Extend the runtime validator** at
   `tests/conformance_robot_envelope_schema.rs`:
   - Add a parallel `every_golden_error_envelope_validates_against_schema`
     test that loads `error_envelope` (or the new error fixtures)
     and validates each against the schema.
   - Add a coverage assertion: every error_code constant in
     production code (`mcp_tools.rs::MCP_ERR_*`) MUST appear
     in at least one fixture (so adding a new error_code
     forces a fixture update).

3. **Production hint redaction**: route every `McpEnvelope::error`
   call through `engine.redact_secrets()` for the `hint` field
   before it leaves the process. Mirror ft-3se13's audit-log fix.

## Out of scope

- The synthetic falsification gates already in ft-5ikbd (they're
  fine; this bead extends real-fixture coverage).
- The MCP coverage matrix (ft-zaqi8 — that's a higher-level
  spec/test-mapping work).

## Acceptance criteria

- ≥10 error envelope fixtures pinned (one per error_code constant
  used in production).
- A new test validates each against the schema.
- The coverage assertion fails red when a new `MCP_ERR_*` constant
  is added without a fixture.
- Production `McpEnvelope::error` redacts the hint field.

## References

- `crates/frankenterm-core/tests/conformance_robot_envelope_schema.rs:116`
  (the success-only fixture loader)
- `crates/frankenterm-core/src/mcp_tools.rs` (`MCP_ERR_*`
  constants)
- `docs/json-schema/wa-robot-envelope.json` (schema covers both
  branches)
- ft-5ikbd (sibling: runtime success-envelope validator —
  already shipped)
- ft-3se13 (sibling: secrets in audit log — same
  redactor-on-write principle applies to hint field)

 {"id":"ft-phz7x","title":"codec: buffered_frame_len accepts oversized tagged_len, allowing memory-exhaustion before MAX_PDU_SIZE fires","description":"frankenterm/codec/src/lib.rs:240 buffered_frame_len validates only that the leb128-decoded payload_len fits in usize. It does NOT check against MAX_PDU_SIZE (256 MiB at lib.rs:106). An attacker who sends a frame whose outer tagged_len advertises e.g. 10 GiB forces try_read_and_decode at lib.rs:763-792 into a read loop that keeps extending the caller's buffer 4 KiB at a time until buffer.len() >= total_len — because stream_decode's first step is if buffered_frame_len(buffer)?.is_none() { return Ok(None); }, which short-circuits to 'need more bytes' and never rejects oversize. decode_raw at lib.rs:395-403 DOES reject via MAX_PDU_SIZE, but by then the process has already OOM'd or swapped.
